trajectory_summary: Rocky is one of the most qualified leads in the corpus — he volunteers kWh data, a prior quote, a named competitor, and a specific disappointment, all unprompted. Brandon books the appointment, which is the right outcome, but the call's energy shape is flat: Rocky's enthusiasm signals were acknowledged but never amplified, and the close contains an ungrounded promise that creates closer-stage risk. The booking is real but fragile — Rocky is comparison-shopping and the appointment holds only as long as no competitor calls him first.
outcome_dispute: v1 outcome tag is unknown. Based on the transcript, the call ends with a confirmed appointment for Thursday the 23rd at 1:00 PM. Outcome should be tagged 'appointment_booked' — but the appointment is conditional on Rocky sending the bill and has no button-up protocol elements (no one-hour block stated, no computer/no-driving anchor, no 'is that fair' handshake). Tag as 'booked_fragile' if that category exists.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Bill-anchor flip — capture without emotional conversion
primary_coaching_focus.quote: In the mail. Yeah. So it's, but I have a copy of it. It's roughly, you know, my average over the last year is somewhere around 380 a month.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Rocky volunteers his monthly bill unprompted and Brandon responds with 'Okay. Great.' and moves to logistics. This is the schema's universal failure pattern on the floor's highest-leverage move. Rocky gave Brandon the number, the frustration context (Sunrun's partial offset), and the emotional opening — all in the first four minutes. Brandon filed the data and moved on. In the 1-on-1, play 03:40 to 04:09 and ask Brandon what he did with the $380 number. The coaching goal is to build the habit of converting bill capture into emotional alignment-against-the-utility before moving to the appointment ask.

key_moments.quote: that there was, a way to get this the Tesla roof tiles. Is that is that the one that you're calling about?
key_moments.stewart_read: Rocky opens with a specific Tesla roof tile expectation — the narrowest version of the expectation gap the schema flags. Brandon's response is a flat denial ('No. This would be the you have a form for solar panels') with no reframe. The schema's correct handling is: acknowledge the referral, clarify Ion is Tesla's installer partner, note that Ion carries its own panels plus the Tesla Powerwall battery, and that the closer walks through options. Brandon skips all of this and pivots to the form. Strategic read: Brandon may have been trying to move past the confusion quickly to avoid scope creep. Reactive read: the flat denial without reframe leaves Rocky's expectation gap unresolved — he never learns what Ion actually offers relative to what he was hoping for.

key_moments.quote: In the mail. Yeah. So it's, but I have a copy of it. It's roughly, you know, my average over the last year is somewhere around 380 a month.
key_moments.stewart_read: Rocky volunteers his average monthly bill unprompted — $380/month — and Brandon responds with 'Okay. Great.' and immediately pivots to explaining what the bill photo will be used for. This is the schema's universal failure pattern verbatim: 'ask + acknowledge + move on.' The $380 number is the emotional anchor for the entire pitch and Brandon treats it as a data point to be filed, not a pain point to be amplified. The flip was available: Rocky's $380/month is the expense solar replaces, not a number to acknowledge and move past.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. I mean, I'll tell you, like, when the sun run, I mean, they were able to only save me they weren't able to save me much. Like, it was yeah. It'll reduce my bill by a little bit, but that's it. So I'm curious to see if if you can offset it.
key_moments.stewart_read: Rocky's Sunrun disappointment is not an objection — it's a buying signal. He's explicitly telling Brandon that the prior experience left him unsatisfied and he's open to a better outcome. This is the highest-leverage moment in the call. Brandon's response ('Yeah. Typically, we want to offset your entire bill') is factually correct but emotionally inert. The flip was available: Rocky's frustration with Sunrun's partial offset is the exact emotional fuel the schema's objection_inversion is designed to use. The move isn't to explain Ion's approach — it's to validate Rocky's frustration and redirect it toward curiosity about what Ion's design will show.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. Typically, we want to offset your entire bill. Because if we can't, then it doesn't make sense for you to go solar.
key_moments.stewart_read: Brandon's answer is scope-appropriate — he correctly defers the actual design and numbers to the specialist rather than quoting savings ranges. The thin answer is the right call. The coaching gap is what's missing after the answer: there's no curiosity bridge to the appointment. Brandon answers Rocky's question and then pauses, waiting for Rocky to move the conversation forward. The setter's job at this moment is to convert Rocky's curiosity into appointment urgency — 'that's exactly what our specialist will show you' — not to answer and wait.
